data.title: Nvidia: 2025 Should Be Strong, But Demand Could Drop In The Years After
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) has been the hottest stock on Wall Street since ChatGPT’s public release in November 2022. Since then, AI has been at the center stage for most major companies. Either they are making their own AI models, or they are looking for ways to integrate AI into their business. Companies have been investing first, while looking for returns later. Nvidia has been the largest winner in the AI space because of their advanced hardware and, most importantly, their CUDA software.

This article covers the growth potential of Blackwell from a technical and financial perspective. But more importantly, I am going to look at the problems I see ahead for Nvidia. The hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers are critical customers for Nvidia, and cracks are appearing in their business model. Finally, I will cover tariffs and what conditions I am looking for to buy Nvidia given the current volatility.

Nvidia’s Extreme Growth

Nvidia’s revenue and margins have grown rapidly because of growth in their data center segment. AI investment began to rapidly increase in 2023. This has led to Nvidia’s data center revenue increasing 6.67X in just two years, from $15B in 2022 (FY23) to $115B in 2024 (FY25).

Data centers are now Nvidia’s primary source of revenue, generating 88.3% of revenue in FY25. Their gaming and professional visualization segments have seen little growth, floating between $10B to $15B a year. The newer automotive segment is also experiencing high growth but only contributed $1.7B in revenue in FY25. I expect the automotive segment will grow substantially in the coming years as their self-driving car platform advances. Additionally, robotics revenue could also become a major contributor.

The strong demand for AI chips has allowed Nvidia to increase their margins. Before AI took off, their margins were in the mid-40% range, but they have increased to 67% in 2024. I doubt Nvidia will be able to increase margins further because higher prices could weaken demand and incentivize customers to use competing chips. Additionally, higher prices from Nvidia will worsen the profitability of hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers; more on this later.

Blackwell, Nvidia’s Most Demanded GPU Yet

Blackwell (GB200) is Nvidia’s next data center GPU, and it will be the most powerful and likely best-selling entry yet. The GB200 started shipping early this year and will continue to ramp through 2025. During Nvidia’s GTC presentation, Jensen shared that 1.8M GB200s are expected to be shipped to the top four hyperscalers this year. Hopper (H100 & H200) peaked at 1.3M units sold in a single year to the top hyperscalers. It is important to note that each GB200 contains two GPUs packaged as a single unit, therefore the 3.6M GPUs from their presentation are 1.8M units.

The GB200 NVL72 rack (36 Grace CPUs and 72 GB200s with NVLink) is expected to cost $3M each. While standalone GB200 + Grace superchips are expected to cost around $70,000. Assuming hyperscalers only purchase racks since that is the most cost and space-efficient, Nvidia could earn $75B in 2025 revenue from just their top four customers. Large customers likely get volume discounts, but even then, Nvidia will generate substantial revenue from them.

Nvidia also unveiled the Blackwell Ultra (GB300) which provides 50% more performance than the GB200. The processor will start shipping in the second half of 2025.

A key driver for Blackwell's demand is its substantial performance improvement over Hopper. Nvidia benchmarks show the GB200 being 25X to 40X more performance than the H100. The actual performance depends on the data type and is shown in the graph below.

Greater processing throughput allows for more tokens to be generated by a single processor. This will be especially important as reasoning models become more widely used. In the initial wave of AI models, the system would directly answer the prompt. In a reasoning system, the AI automatically re-prompts itself to mimic human reasoning. The more the model thinks, the more tokens it will generate, leading to more computation demand.

Nvidia is betting that greater token production capabilities will lead to more complicated models. At GTC they also teased the performance capabilities of their next GPU, Rubin, which has 13X the performance of Blackwell (see slide 38). Nvidia needs AI model complexity to increase in line with hardware performance improvements. Otherwise, they could end up cannibalizing their future revenues. This would be good for customers but bad for Nvidia.

Decline in GPU Rental Rates

During Jensen’s GTC keynote, he stated that “when Blackwell starts shipping in volume you couldn’t give Hoppers away.” He tried to walk the statement back, but he is 100% correct given the performance improvements. This leads to the first major problem I see Nvidia facing in the future.

Nvidia’s data center customers can be divided into three groups. Hyperscalers are the large cloud providers, including Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT). Then there are alternative cloud providers that are mostly AI-focused, such as CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV). Finally, there are direct customers who purchase the GPUs for their own use, such as Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). It is especially important to look at hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers because they are speculatively buying Nvidia’s GPUs to rent to AI developers. Disruptions in the rental market will eventually impact Nvidia, and the first cracks are starting to appear.

When demand for H100 chips first began to take off, one of Nvidia’s sales tactics was promoting the high ROI cloud providers could earn. At the time, an H100 could be rented for $4/hour or more. For every dollar invested in Nvidia’s hardware, the cloud provider could earn $5 in rent over 4 years. In 2023, this looked like a great investment. Demand for AI chips had outstripped supply and demand looked like it could only go up. This led to the large investment in AI data center build outs by hyperscalers and alternative cloud providers seen in Nvidia’s large revenue growth.

However, unrelenting demand and supply constraints have come to an end. GPU rental rates have been dropping for some time now, and only hyperscalers have been able to keep their margins high. AWS and Azure still charge around $8/hour for H100s, but alternative cloud providers have had to cut their rates. H100s can be rented for under $2/hour now with most alternative cloud providers charging less than $4/hour.

An excellent data center cost breakdown by Latent Space shows just how precarious a position the alternative cloud providers are in. When looking at the total cost of ownership (infrastructure, GPUs, CPUs, switches, electricity, etc.) the 5-year breakeven price for an H100 is $1.65/hour. To earn a 10% annual return, rental prices must exceed $2.85/hour. These rental rates also assume 100% utilization. Longer idle times waiting for a customer to use the chip increase the required rental rates.

The chart below provides the rates of return and payback times that can be expected for various rental rates and electricity prices. If the rental rate continues to drop it will be unlikely that newly purchased H100s will ever be profitable.

Additionally, Blackwell will likely drive down H100 rental prices due to the substantial performance improvements it provides. Looking at CoreWeave’s pricing, a H100 rents for $6.16/hour while a GB200 rents for $21/hour. For equal performance, a GB200 provides 7.33X more tokens per dollar; assuming the GB200 produces 25X as many tokens as a H100.

As Blackwell becomes more available it’s a no-brainer which chip a company developing or deploying its AI should use. This could result in substantial idle H100 inventory and significant losses for cloud providers that were unable to recoup their investment. Many of the alternative cloud providers are venture capital backed. Though VC funds seem to love throwing money at loss-making companies, it is only a matter of time before the money dries up. This could result in bankruptcies and fewer sales of Nvidia’s future hardware since there are fewer customers to buy their chips. The decreasing rental rates also show a decreased appetite for AI capacity relative to supply. This means Nvidia could be forced to cut their prices to find new willing buyers.

Pull Forward of Demand Due to Large Data Center Build Out

Another concern I have about investing in Nvidia is the probability of a significant pull forward in revenue. If my pull-forward worry is correct, then Nvidia’s current revenue is higher than what it will be long term. I don’t believe Nvidia will go back to pre-AI levels, but I also don’t believe they will be able to maintain current revenues forever. Nvidia should be able to keep revenue high this year, but within the next few years I expect a peak in revenue will be clearly visible.

Nvidia’s revenue increase has mirrored growth in data centers. CBRE Group, Inc. (CBRE) research into data center development expects deliveries in MW capacity to peak in 2024. There have been large data center plans announced since the report was produced, but there have also been cancellations.

The Stargate Project between OpenAI, SoftBank Group Corp. (OTCPK:SFTBY), Oracle Corporation (ORCL), and the UAE MGX investment fund plans to invest $500B over the next four years in building AI data centers. At the same time, Microsoft has backed out of 2GW of planned data center additions. Microsoft is likely pulling back their build out because they are allowing OpenAI to use other providers besides Azure. Stargate will likely backfill the void left by Microsoft. This brings up another concern about new data center developments. Are the large data center announcements adding to previously forecast growth or is it merely a change in who is writing the check.

Improving Efficiency

The decline in new data center capacity could be offset by greater chip efficiency. Nvidia has made great strides in the amount of processing per MW. If efficiency gains are as great as Nvidia projects, then we could be at peak data center build out, but that doesn't mean we are at peak chip demand. Instead, more servers could be located at existing data centers without the need for more power.

I am a structural engineer, and some of the projects I work on are switching stations (small data centers) for a major telecom company. Many of our projects have involved reworking their existing data centers to convert office space into data halls. Since the servers they use have become more power efficient, they can have more servers at the same site without bringing in more power. This saves them significant time and money by not having to build new data centers.

The clash between improved performance and excessive data center build out will leave someone holding the bag. Either speculative data center developers providing space no one requires, or cloud providers fully fitting out data centers only to find insufficient customer demand and lots of idle servers.

I am not alone in this observation. Alibaba Group Holding Limited’s (BABA) chairman called the large AI build out a bubble, with investors “investing ahead of the demand that they’re seeing today.”

AI growth has been pitched as an exponential demand, but exponential demand never lasts. In a similar fashion to Moore’s Law, Huang’s Law states that the performance of GPUs will more than double every two years. Nvidia is doing a wonderful job beating this performance growth rate. In a world where GPU performance grows exponentially, there is no reason to expect data center demand will also grow exponentially.

Impact of Tariffs

The preceding analysis didn’t account for tariffs. Semiconductors are currently exempt, but President Trump has said they are coming soon.

As a reminder, tariffs are paid on the value of the imported product, not the sales price.

Nvidia uses Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) to produce their chips in Taiwan, but the actual supply chain is even more complicated. Nvidia doesn’t usually sell their GPUs directly to the end customer. Instead, they sell GPUs to an OEM that assembles the chips into servers.

A large portion of the server assembly capacity is in Southeast Asian countries. For now, it is safe to assume that semiconductor tariffs will be similar to the recently enacted tariffs on other goods. The enacted tariffs vary from 24% in Malaysia to 46% in Vietnam.

Hopefully, semiconductors will remain exempt from tariffs due to the large amount already being invested to create new fabs within the US, but this seems unlikely given Trump’s comments. If tariffs are enacted, customers will have to pay more for new servers or OEMs will have to accept lower profit margins. In either case, I expect pressure will be applied to Nvidia to cut prices to help offset any tariff.

Tariffs have come on and off quite rapidly, but I believe this round will be stickier. Investors should closely monitor any future semiconductor tariff announcements because they will hurt Nvidia’s revenue. Either through fewer units sold or lower profit margins. Based on Trump’s reaction to the stock market meltdown, I am assuming tariffs will remain, but the tariff rate might be lowered.

Is Nvidia Worth Investing In

I believe the initial scarcity of AI compute was the primary reason the AI bubble was able to take off. If chips had been more readily available, I doubt the frantic investment in everything AI would have happened. Big data, machine learning, and AI are the same thing, only the marketing spin is different.

What matters most is determining where Nvidia’s revenues are headed because that will determine long-term value. Hype can boost P/E multiples, while fundamentals determine what happens when the hype wears off.

Blackwell should be a major revenue source for Nvidia. If orders don’t get canceled due to tariffs, 2025 should be Nvidia’s highest revenue year yet. Blackwell has a higher price than Hopper, and Blackwell is expected to deliver more units this year than Hopper’s peak year. This is a compelling reason to invest in Nvidia. However, it is unwise to believe Nvidia’s growth streak will continue forever.

Nvidia’s future profitability is dependent on continued AI investments. Falling rental prices indicate abundance, not scarcity. If data center providers are having to cut their rates to find customers, I doubt they will be in a rush to purchase even more chips. Nvidia’s backlog is robust today, but I expect it will fall in the future as current orders are delivered into a weakening server market.

The slowing of data center build out will also slow potential revenue growth and may lead to contractions. New data centers require new servers. Existing data centers will eventually have to refresh their servers, but cloud providers will stretch their update cycles to meet customer demand and their own financial needs.

Initially, when I started working on this article, I was going to give Nvidia a buy rating because of strong Blackwell orders. However, tariffs and market turmoil make buying right now inadvisable. I am giving Nvidia a hold rating for now, but I will outline what I am looking for to make Nvidia a buy.

Current Market Volatility & When to Buy

Nvidia’s share price has been declining since DeepSeek's, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK), DeepSeek-R1 showed how AI models could be made more efficient. Recent market turmoil has only accelerated the sell-off. The current market uncertainty is creating a compelling buying opportunity, and the further Nvidia falls the greater the potential gain. The chart below shows the last 10 years of Nvidia’s trailing twelve-month GAAP P/E. This is the true measure of Nvidia’s performance and eliminates biases in forward estimate based P/Es.

Looking at their P/E history, Nvidia is currently trading at a better value than the pullback in 2022. Only during the rate hiking cycle in 2018 has Nvidia’s P/E fallen lower. 2018 was also a year marked by tariff uncertainty. Since 2018 is rhyming with 2025, I believe it is best to use it as a guide.

Nvidia has a trailing twelve-month diluted EPS of $2.94 per share. Nvidia’s P/E bottomed in January 2019 at around 17.0X, this would imply a price target of $50 per share. Since timing the market is impossible, I am targeting an entry under a 20X P/E, which is $59 per share. Below this threshold, Nvidia becomes a worthwhile investment if the downtrend no longer looks like a falling knife.

Alternatively, if the tariff drama is resolved—truly, not just speculation about a deal—and markets stabilize, then Nvidia instantly becomes a buy, no matter its price. Some market participants are going risk-off, which has been visible since DeepSeek-R1 came out. Others are hanging on every news drop hoping for recovery. What matters is the macro trajectory, not single-day swings. Once the market has redirected upwards Nvidia becomes a buy, but don’t chase rapid rises on hope.

I view Nvidia as a trade. The potential near-term return is great once markets stabilize, and the further Nvidia falls the better upside potential there is. In the long term, I plan to watch data center construction and AI demand. Any slowing in the growth narrative will be bad for Nvidia’s stock.

data.title: Nvidia: Tariffs Have Strengthened My Conviction (Rating Upgrade To Strong Buy)
data.description:
Scott Bessent on re-shoring plans

Having last covered Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ: NVDA) ()not long ago, the destruction in broad market equity prices since the time of that article has put stocks on an even a bigger sale than they were at the time.

My logic at the time was as follows:

In growth stories like Nvidia, I look at the PEG ratio in one of two ways, I either draw up a modified PEG ratio adding back research and development expense [R&D], figure how much adjusted op income they have per share, and then multiply the per share adjusted op income by the growth rate, capping it at 25 X [this assumes a max long-term growth rate of 25%.

Or I use the traditional method of looking at the forward expected growth rate, using that as the multiplier times the next year's expected GAAP EPS as the multiplicand. Again, I cap this at 25X, so a stock needs to grow earnings at least at a higher clip than 25% expected to earn my upper-end multiplier. Again, with PEG, a ratio of 1 or less is a fair value to a bargain, depending on how low below a 1 ratio it goes.

As Peter Lynch famously stated (emphasis added):

In general, a p/e ratio that's half the growth rate is very positive, and one that's twice the growth rate is very negative. Lynch, Peter. One Up On Wall Street. 1989 (199)

What has the growth rate in earnings been in recent years? (My favorites are those in the 20 to 25 percent range. I'm wary of companies that seem to be growing faster than 25 percent. Those 50 percenters usually are found in hot industries, and you know what that means.) Lynch, Peter. One Up On Wall Street. 1989 (229).

Taking this into consideration, Nvidia stock looks like a steal should growth rates hold up, and the company be less affected by tariffs. Right now, the company is trading at a forward GAAP P/E ratio of 22.61 X and a very low PEG ratio even if buyers of their product cut CAPEX due to price increases in data centers related to tariffs by quite a bit. There's really no alternative to their GPUs at the moment.

The crux of my argument here to upgrade this to a strong buy [not that I'm the only one of that opinion here] is the exact same negative catalyst that has been crashing the market, tariffs. Re-shoring, or bringing manufacturing back to U.S. territory via tariff threats seems to be the modus operandi of action for the Trump administration for now and the highlight of both Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's belief systems.

Treasury Secretary Bessent has been making the rounds eloquently explaining the thought process behind the tariffs, the rationale for bringing the US supply chain of many critical manufactured goods back under control, and how they plan to do it. They believe that smart factories, robotics, and AI could be the key to driving down US manufacturing costs, putting them on par with some of the lowest cost of human capital countries [especially when you include tariffs].

Elon Musk and Tesla (TSLA) have [or at least had] figured out how to produce cars in the U.S. with some of the best margins in the world. It is possible, and I'm sure Musk has championed this possibility behind closed doors. You only pick up these efficiencies by having more AI spend, more AI-related supply chain management systems, and more robotics. Nvidia is the obvious necessary component in this whole scenario, and I would argue it is now becoming a protected U.S. National Security asset to the level of a Boeing (BA) or Lockheed Martin (LMT).

I can only imagine the level of support they will receive in upcoming years as Southeast Asia manufacturing comes to them with investment commitments from Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). Yes, tariffs for building the chips in Taiwan will cause margins to compress for one of those companies or both, but I assume Taiwan will receive some favorability in negotiations which are already being lined up.

Can you tariff the cloud?

When we talk about Nvidia, yes they make a physical product for domestic use or export, and yes, a retaliatory tariff from a foreign country could make their products more expensive and curb demand. I am going to argue that GPUs go into data centers, data centers are accessed through the cloud, and a GPU could be inserted and used in a more favorable tariff country by an adjacent country with less favorable tariffs.

The same goes for nations that have export bans for Nvidia's products. Through a shell of a shell or a JV of a JV, countries with limited access to Nvidia's products will find access to the product whether the U.S. likes it or not. The cloud is a reality and U.S. adversaries could be spinning servers in Cupertino, California for all we know. If the demand for worldwide compute continues to increase, there are few bans, tariffs or tactics that could limit the demand for Nvidia product in my opinion.

The U.S. would be better off focusing on building the best AI systems that they control, rather than wasting time trying to keep the GPUs out of the hands of others. Additionally, whatever retaliatory tariffs come from other countries, check their schedules, I would doubt they would put Draconian tariffs on critical computer chips that they import from U.S. companies, it would be tantamount to developmental suicide.

Year-to-date performance

In my last article on Nvidia, the company had the second-worst year-to-date performance of the Mag 7, but now Apple (AAPL) has come up the rear and butted them out of the way for 2nd place [or second worst]. Regardless, Apple and Tesla do not seem to be anywhere near as good of a deal as Nvidia, let's take a look at the forward P/E ratios:

Nvidia sits 3rd cheapest on the pile for a forward P/E multiple; however, they also have the highest anticipated forward earnings growth rate. As of current, it's either Alphabet (GOOGL) or Nvidia that has the most attractive valuation in my opinion. Alphabet on an absolute basis and Nvidia on a growth rate to multiple basis. Both are at the top of my list of Mag 7 buys in this 2022 recap of the tech bear market.

The balance sheet is still amazing

The lines for Nvidia on the balance sheet are all going in the right direction. EBITDA is way up and to the right, cash and short-term investments are on the same trajectory, and long-term debt is a blip compared to EBITDA at less than 1/10th [companies should be able to leverage up to 3 X EBITDA if needed]. Shares outstanding have been dropping dramatically, this is certainly a company that is both self-financing and believes in itself. I hope management puts some extra cash to work on this downturn.

The patterns look like a fortunate astrology chart. Mars is in Aries. Love it.

Risks and summary

Nvidia has several risks, but at least financial solvency is not one of those issues like it might be for Intel (INTC), for example. China is breathing down Taiwan's neck in the midst of a heated trade war. Taiwan controls the Fabless company's GPU supply, and the U.S.-capable FABs cannot come online soon enough. Every report of a canceled data center lease by Microsoft (MSFT) or another software hyperscaler rattles the market. Those same hyperscalers are also trying to make competing LLM-capable processors.

Regardless of the above, I recommend any who have not watched Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's recent round of interviews, especially the one with Tucker Carlson that was over an hour, to have a watch. They truly believe U.S. manufacturing can return, even for small electronic goods. He frequently cites AI and robotics as the way to compress costs.

An example of this might be Red Cat (RCAT), a small Drone company that has products that will replace DJI Chinese drones in the U.S. military and is made in the U.S. state of Utah. A small, low-value-added product. The main way they intend to generate positive margins is through AI-related supply chain management with Palantir (PLTR). It will be worth watching the margins of the low-value add manufacturers more than others because if you can figure out a way to build a small electronic device profitably in the U.S., then we are quickly approaching the point at which AI ROI is a proven fact. At that point, GPU demand only accelerates from there.

Strong buy.

data.title: Driving Drunk Without Brakes
data.description:
I’ve continuously pointed out the danger of asset bubbles because they commonly lead to a slippery slope of destructive behaviours, threatening the health and stability of individuals, businesses, and the economy.

After a dramatic wipeout in March 2020, stock prices rebounded into December 2021 before plunging into October 2022. Rebounding again, some sectors finally reclaimed their December 2021 high by January 2024; a few made a fresh high in early 2025, but that, too, was short-lived.

As of this morning, the widely held S&P 500 is -4% year over year and just 6.4% above its December 2021 peak. The tech-rich Nasdaq, which inspired so much mania, is -4.9% over the past year and -3.2% since November 2021.

Canada’s TSX, flat over the past year, is +5% since November 2021.

The economically sensitive small-cap S&P 600 index has fallen 27% since Trump’s election and is down 21% since November 2021.

In short, irrationally overvalued stock markets have performed as history promised, with a wild ride of volatility and miserably insufficient compensation.

Making returns even worse, participants have paid higher management and brokerage fees for the ‘risk-on’ experience, often while taking ill-advised withdrawal rates.

The bad news is that still-elevated valuations suggest further mean reversion is to come.

Continuing to take sober measurements and manage capital responsibly through full market cycles is mentally demanding, and can be unappreciated and mocked during bubbles. In a world full of bad financial advice and get-rich-quick schemes, it’s hard for the masses to steer clear.

Looking for fast-moving vehicles, many jump in with confident drunk drivers who have no brakes. It’s only after inevitable crashes that they see the harm.

I reviewed the setup for the current loss cycle in a Thoughtful Money video with Adam Taggart last November; see, Stocks are the most overvalued in 100 years.

It is never too late to learn from mistakes, but recovery requires enlightenment and resolve to make wiser choices. Yes, we can.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Prudential plc: A Contender To Add To My Portfolio, But Not During This Volatility
data.description:
Introduction

Prudential plc (NYSE:PUK) recently reported its full-year 2024 earnings results, so I wanted to take a look at its performance throughout the year as well as give some of my thoughts on the company’s potential in the short term as well as some of my worries.

Briefly on performance

Looking at the company’s top-line growth, we can see it has accelerated from Q3 onward in 2024. This strength in sales can be attributed to the Annual Premium Equivalent sales growing by 10% y/y with agency and bancassurance channels doing the hard work. Growth in China and Hong Kong was particularly strong at the end of the year, with APE sales to domestic customers growing a whopping 36% y/y.

Let’s also look at the company’s profitability and efficiency metrics for the year. Gross margins are not as important to insurance companies as for many other companies, so I would like to focus on operating margins and net margins, which have seen a massive recovery since hitting the bottom at the end of Q2 ’24. The shift of focus on less capital-intensive, and higher-margin products in key markets like China contributed massively to such an astounding improvement to the company’s profitability. There was also an economic recovery in Asia, which ignited consumer demand for insurance and retirement planning products.

Continuing with efficiency and profitability, the company’s return on equity, which measures how well the management is utilizing the shareholder capital to create profit, has recovered handsomely since Q2 ’24 also, and is slightly above the industry average, although only slightly. Nevertheless, above average is a good sign that the management is doing something right. We can see that it has close to doubled in just a year, and there doesn’t seem to be a pause in sight.

Now onto the liquidity and solvency of the company. Prudential finished the year with $5.7B in cash and equivalents, as well as $162B in total investments. The company has very little debt compared to its assets, so there are no liquidity issues here. The company is also making over $4B in operating profit, so it is very profitable. The company’s Group-Wide Supervision or GWS ratio has been consistently very high, meaning under the GWS framework, which is governed by the Hong Kong Insurance Authority, the company has more than enough capital to cover any sort of economic or operational shocks. The GWS ratio is 280%. The additional capital under the GWS framework can be deployed to further the growth of the company.

Overall, the company had a good 2024, especially in the second half of the year, with massive improvements in sales growth, as well as efficiency and profitability. The management did a commendable job and that has been reflected in the company’s share price which hasn’t been hit as much as many others or even the overall market indices during this unprecedented market volatility.

Comments on the Outlook

I like the fact that the company is not highly exposed to the U.S. markets in general. Most of the company’s investments are in the Asian and African markets. These markets are rapidly growing market and known as emerging markets, which typically means there will be a higher potential for high growth and higher returns. However, since the numbers given right now are as of December 31st ’24, when the markets were still rather hitting record highs, which made the company’s investment returns look very attractive. Now, fast-forward to today, with the tariff war uncertainties wreaking havoc on the global equity and bond markets, I am expecting quite a bit of decline in the next quarter’s results. In just a few days, the new Trump Administration went aggressively on worldwide tariffs, which sent the global markets down into a bear market territory, with Japan taking a massive hit, as well as many other countries, in which PUK heavily invests.

So, that’s not going to be positive for the company. However, most of the company’s revenues come from the insurance side of things. The second half of the year 2024 was fantastic in terms of the strength of the demand for the company’s insurance products. The focus on mass-affluent, and affluent customers across the Asian, and African regions is a very good strategy for growth because Africa’s insurance penetration rate is around 3%, just like in Asia, which means there is a lot of room for growth here and since the company has been doing an amazing job at getting people to sign up in the last two quarters, there is a good chance this will continue going forward. It is evident that there is a lot of demand, which is still rising for retirement solutions.

I would like to see the company not drop the ball here going forward and continue to expand into other uninsured markets in the future to continue to grow its top line and profitability in the future.

On the other hand, since there is a high chance of a global financial crisis, the demand may not continue to be this strong in 2025. People may be a lot more hesitant to start a retirement portfolio when they see that the markets are tanking right now, with no end in sight. People will be more likely to save money in cash and not in a portfolio account right now and in the near future because they don’t want to get involved in such a mess that we see right now. This may also mean there will be a lot more policy lapses as people will prioritize other parts of expenses and push insurance premiums to the side, leading to weaker performance for PUK.

Closing Comments

All of this may not happen, but it will be very interesting to hear what the management has to say about the immediate future as well as if we are going to hear a shift in tone to a more cautious outlook. For this reason, I am going to remain on the sidelines for Prudential plc until we get a clearer sense of where the world is going and how all of this uncertainty will affect the company’s operations.

If the worst-case scenario doesn’t come to fruition, I see a very enticing opportunity coming up over the next few quarters and would be seriously considering starting a small position in PUK, however, since the uncertainty is overwhelming right now, I cannot seem to pull a trigger for a new holding in my portfolio. There are companies that are down in the dirt in my portfolio and will be looking to add to those first as they will be my priority over the next couple of quarters.

data.title: AIPI: A Well-Constructed Fund With Some Elevated Risk
data.description:
The best investments should be able to stand the test of time. While many companies and funds perform well in favorable market conditions, adversity often shows weaknesses more clearly. The market has sold off in an orderly way, but equities have still fallen notably in a short period of time since President Trump unveiled his long-term strategy of using tariffs to renegotiate trade agreements and bring manufacturing back to the United States.

A particular investment that has been impacted significantly by the recent increase in overall market volatility levels has been covered-call funds. These have become more commonplace and evolved significantly over the last decade, and the payouts and total returns these investments offer vary more than most equities during sell-offs.

One well-constructed new covered-call fund that came to market recently in June 2024 is the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:AIPI).

AIPI has offered investors total returns of negative 8.19 percent since the fund's inception in June 2024. The S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of negative 3.16 percent during this same time period, and the Nasdaq 100 has offered investors total returns of negative 6.41 percent since June 2024.

Before the recent sell-off, AIPI also performed essentially in line with the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100, while markets were rising as well.

Today, I am initiating coverage of the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF with a buy rating. This technology-based fund has performed well since the fund's inception in June 2024 in both more favorable market conditions and during the recent sell-off. AIPI uses an effective options strategy that sells out-of-the-money calls, and the fund focuses on large-cap tech companies that should continue to outperform the broader indexes for multiple reasons.

AIPI has an expense ratio of .65 percent, $238 million in assets under management, and a trailing yield of 36.31 percent. The fund seeks to track the BITA AI Leaders Select Index, which is composed of companies that have a high relative amount of revenue from artificial intelligence activities and services. AIPI's top holdings are NVIDIA (NVDA), Palantir Technologies Inc. Ordinary Shares - Class A (PLTR), ARM Holdings PLC ADR (ARM), and CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. Class A (CRWD). These four positions make up nearly 36 percent of the fund's overall holdings. AIPI is a large-cap tech fund.

The strategy that AIPI uses is to sell slightly out-of-the-money monthly call options against all of the fund's equity positions every month. The ETF adjusts the strike price of the options contracts sold based on market conditions.

The strategy of selling out-of-the-money calls has been a more effective strategy used by covered-call funds to minimize risks to the net asset value of these types of funds. Exchange-traded funds such as the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Core Premium Income ETF (GPIX) that sell out-of-the-money calls have offered investors better overall returns by minimizing losses to the principal with this strategy that enables investors to capture more of the upside of the underlying equity holdings.

AIPI is a large-cap tech fund that has performed largely in-line with the Nasdaq 100 during market rallies, and the ETF has only slightly performed tech-based indexes such as the Nasdaq 100 during sell-offs. Covered-call funds are not designed to outperform equities long-term, most income investors are willing to sacrifice on overall returns to get consistent and substantive income.

AIPI has paid out both significant and consistent income since the fund's inception in June 2024. The ETF's payouts have ranged from $1.28 a share to $1.51 a share. This investment's monthly payouts should remain high in the current more volatile market as while since the options premiums are up.

All investments have risks, and AIPI is a new fund with limited data that is not well-diversified. The ETF's top 4 holdings are nearly 36 percent of the fund, and large-cap tech has more international exposure to the current trade conflict right now in addition to this sector's significant operations in China. Still, big-cap tech has outperformed most of the major indexes over the last decade, and corporate balance sheets remain strong, these companies will likely keep spending to increase efficiency.

With increasing signs that the economy is weakening and Trump taking a hardline on trade, markets should remain volatile in the near term. While excessive levels of volatility can hurt principal, Trump is using tariffs to renegotiate longer-term trade agreements and I believe big cap tech earnings have held up relatively well even during periods of weakness. While AIPI won't likely outperform the broader indexes, this fund should continue to offer consistent income and solid total returns.

data.title: WesBanco: Acquisition Completed And Slightly Undervalued
data.description:
Thesis

I rate WesBanco, Inc. (NASDAQ:WSBC) as a "Buy," mainly because of the recent "Tariff Selloff" that made the stock trade at 8.4x Price to Earnings Fwd (Non-Gaap). I see potential upside of 20% for the next 12 months. I also think there may be some merger synergies due to cost savings, but I'm a little bit more skeptical about that. Also, it's performing slightly better than its peers. Today, April 7th, is ranked 48th out of 685 in Financials on Seeking Alpha and 13th out of 250 in Regional Banks. That implies that WesBanco has a higher score than 93% of financials and more than 95% of regional banks. Quant score right now is 4.74, rating it as a "Strong Buy".

As we can see in the quant summary, it's screaming a "BUY" or even a "STRONG BUY" but there are two issues: one is profitability. Return on Equity is 5.62% when the same industry median is at 10.44%.

The second issue is the book value. In some websites, for example, in the Seeking Alpha's valuation section, price-to-book is around 0.7x, but on YCharts and others, the same ratio is around 1x.

The reason for this difference is that the former book value does not take into account the issuing of shares to buy Premier Financial. For each stock owned of Premier Financial, they received 0.8 of WesBanco. Total outstanding shares of WesBanco, prior to the acquisition, were 66,919,805. Now they are 95,670,246, implying an increase of 42%.

Business Overview and Latest Financial Results

WesBanco, Inc. is a diversified financial holding, and "WesBanco Bank" is its primary subsidiary. Founded in 1870, it offers different kinds of financial services, mainly banking, but also wealth management and insurance services. If we follow the general approach to banks, we may separate their two segments in revenues: Net interest income and Non-interest income. The first one represents 80% on average for the last 10 years.

When we look at revenues in more detail, despite the loan growth of 4.15% CAGR, revenues have been flat in the last 5 years.

However, on August 1, 2023, Jeffrey H. Jackson became the new CEO and president of the company. He had previously joined WesBanco as Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer on August 15, 2022. Eleven months after that, the company proposes a merger. Maybe the winds of change are coming, and we will see some new growth in revenues ahead.

From a different perspective, the company, in its 10-K, classifies its segments into two: "community banking" and "trust and investment services".

The community banking segment accounts for 98% of total revenues and 105.6% of profit margin. In this case, is above the total profit margin because the "corporate other" segment contains expenses that belong to the parent company, and since this segment doesn't generate income on its own, the net result is negative. The Trust & Investment segment represents 4.4% of total revenues and 1.3% of total profits (both percentages, averaging the last 3 years).

A red flag that we can note is that the profit margin is falling, from 32% in 2022 to 26% in 2024.

However, not all is gloomy here. On February 28th, the company completed the acquisition of Premier Financial. It was a tender offer since it was approved by 85% of WesBanco's stockholders and 68% of Premier Financial's stockholders. I will delve into it in the merger analysis section.

Total loans grew 8.8% YoY. The core section, like most banks, is the commercial real estate, which includes "Land and construction" and "Improved property," representing 57.8% of total Loans and growing at 11.5% YoY. Land and Construction is the star sector, growing 28%, and 'consumer loans' is the worst, falling by 12.3%.

When we perform an age analysis of loans, we can notice two things. The first is that in 2023, total past due represented 0.43% of total loans, and at the end of 2024, that percentage rose to 0.7%, which implies an increase of 78% YoY of total past due loans, or 27 basis points.

Second, the two segments that have increased their past due portion are "Improved property" and "Commercial and Industrial" ones, and for the former, its past-due loans have been increasing in the last 90 days, for the 2024 fiscal year.

At first glance, the numbers look fine if we take into account the national delinquency mean of 3.6% for Q4 2024. Here, delinquency is defined as 90 days or more.

The company is an outstanding place to work, according to the polls of Newsweek. Nearly 95 percent said their work is "fulfilling", and approximately 90 percent said they had recommended WesBanco as a "great place to work" to people outside the organization.

Also, in different metrics, the company has shown to be better than its peers in its asset quality.

When it comes to interest rates, the market has changed a lot in the last days. A week ago, the market was pricing a probability of only 15.6% for the FED to cut down the rates to the 4.00-4.25 range in the next meeting on May 7th; now the probability is 46.1%. Also, if you look further in time, for example, for December 17th, 2025, the implicit interest rate in the market price is around 3.5%.

Interest rates are critical in banks that are "asset-sensitive". When the interest rates rise, banks can reprice the new loans, and if they don't reprice at the same speed as the deposits, the Net Interest Margin [NIM] can improve, and that's the reason that, in most cases, rising rates are a pretty good thing for banks. The opposite may be true in some cases, falling rates put some pressure on the NIM, and banks struggle more to be profitable. In short, the macroeconomic context doesn't shed any light on WesBanco as other banks and companies.

In the last earnings call, the CFO claimed that the assets of WesBanco are pretty much neutral to changes in interest rates. I think that could be true to a great extent, but we should remain aware of the macroeconomic context. Also, they are modelling in their guidance and pro-forma earnings two more cuts of the FED. So, if that's the case, two more cuts may not be a big deal, but I insist, we should be concerned about that.

Having said that, they are anticipating 4 to 6 basis points of improvement on the NIM, for Q1 2025 and a little bit more for Q2 2025, due that 1 billion of Certificates of Deposits are maturing, and they expect to reprice them at a higher rate.

Merger Analysis

According to Prof. Damodaran, "Acquisitions are great for target companies, but not always for acquiring company stockholders".

That is precisely what happened with Premier Financial Corp, the target company in this case. The stock price soared between 30% and 40% in the days before the announcement of the acquisition on July 26th.

According to Prof. Damodaran, there are only 3 cases when the acquisition/merger creates value.

The first is operating synergies, like economies of scale (eg, both companies can dispense with several installations and use the same one for both companies, saving costs). Another one is the economics of scope, maybe some services exist in one company but not in the other, and they can begin offering these services to both, and that may deliver some additional growth.

A second way is financial synergies, combined companies may have a higher capacity to borrow debt, and they may use it to expand the business. Also, in some cases, they have tax benefits.

The third way is when the target company is potentially undervalued. The acquisition can be a driver that may lead to a more reasonable valuation, meanwhile, shareholders of the bidder company benefit from the rise in prices.

In this case, the argument put forth by management is that they expect to have operating synergies, more specifically, saving costs. Both management and analysts (consensus) are expecting around a 40% rise in EPS for 2025 and a 17% rise for 2026.

Here we can see it in more detail.

Delving deep into Pro-Forma EPS for 2025, management expects a 42% accretion, which implies a P/E of 7.73 for 2025. To get this number, they are excluding one-time charges (27.1 million) and fully phased in the Run-Rate saving costs (31 million). However, despite being a "one-time charge", the company will have to pay for them inevitably, and we don't know for sure how much time it would take to save these 31.5 million in costs. Alternatively, a more realistic case is the 27% accretion that they are expecting, which gives us a P/E of 8.57 for 2025.

I'm somewhat concerned about the "Fair Market Value" adjustments. They represent 68% of the EPS accretion expected and 22% of the pro forma earnings. However, this may not be something to worry about, but we should be aware of it and the magnitude of it. Banks have to do very often these kinds of adjustments, to reflect the current conditions of the market, especially interest rates.

Wrapping up, my opinion on the merger is slightly positive, not as strong as management and analysts expect. Combining the two companies can help save some costs, it is highly reasonable. However, on the other hand, the acquisition, as well as any other, carries some risks, for example, that the two companies' teams don't get along very well with each other. But, as of now, it's a potential problem, and if it occurs, it can be handled. Having stated that, I think that the "small" benefits of the acquisition outweigh the risks of it.

Valuation

Looking at the price to book ratio, it's average, in both ways compared with its peers, but also on a historical basis. Regarding price to book, as I discussed in the thesis section, we are going to see in some places something around 0.7x, but the right levels are around 1x.

The different peer companies that I used to compare to WesBanco are: Peoples Bancorp (PEBO), TowneBank (TOWN), First Commonwealth Financial Corporation (FCF), Trustmark Corporation (TRMK), and Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB). They are regional banks and have around the same market cap.

And the other bad issue about this bank is, Return on Equity is substantially below its peers.

The only ratio that can seem appealing is P/E forward, in both ways, when compared to peers and compared to history.

Compared to its peers is something interesting, but when compared to the historical average, it shows a strong potential undervaluation. But keep in mind that the EPS used is assuming an optimistic view of the acquisition, where the cost savings are successful, and also the fair market adjustments are "realistic".

As a rule of thumb, if P/E forward reverses to the 10-year historical mean of 12.8x from its current level of 8.4x, we can expect a 52% upside. But if we take the 3-year historical mean of 11.4x, the upside is 35%.

Obviously, these targets are very "spicy", and they are based on the premise of a successful EPS accretion, which I'm not fully convinced.

Risks

There are several topics that we can list as risks, for example, commercial real estate exposure, loan quality deterioration, mark-to-market losses, changes in regulations, etc, but the 3 main risks that I consider the most critical right now are: probability of recession, flattening or inverting of the yield curve and deposit outflows.

Goldman Sachs just hiked the odds of recession for the next year to 45%, J. P. Morgan even a starker 60% probability, and the New York FED Model is showing 30% at the end of March.

Concerning the yield curve, maybe right now it is not so flat or even inverted as, for example, one year ago. But the near short-term portion that includes between a couple of months and up to 2 years is already inverted. This curve is critical to banks, due that they usually borrow at a short term and lower rate, and then they may lend money at a longer term and higher rate. If the curve gets inverted, it puts a lot of pressure on the Net Income Margin and compromises profitability.

Regarding the risk of deposit outflows, we can take, for example, the bank crisis of March-May 2023. Several banks have suffered from several problems, but one critical issue was the withdrawal of deposits. In the following picture, we can note the drop in total deposits. If we take June 2022 to June 2023, the drop was 3.8% for all commercial banks in the US. But when it comes to individual banks, some of them didn't make it, and others were very complicated.

In the case of WesBanco, total deposits fell 5.21% in the same period. Interest-bearing deposits rose 1.7%, non-interest-bearing fell 9.5%, and institutional deposits dropped an astonishing 75%. This didn't move the needle because institutional deposits only represented 1-3% of total deposits, but that was not a good sign from the institutional.

Having said that, after analyzing the company, I don't find big problems or risks "out of the norm" inside the company, but I note the macroeconomic and systemic risks and their possible impact on WesBanco.

Conclusion

Wrapping up, in my opinion, it's a solid Bank, but it's not growing as fast and doesn't have the profitability that I would like. However, there are some reasons, like the acquisition and the new CEO, that make me give it a chance to the company, and maybe we can expect better figures regards to growth and profitability. As I said before, I'm a little bit skeptical about the synergies, and I'm also concerned about the macroeconomic context.

I'm going to value this company in two different ways, first using P/E Non-GAAP Fwd and Price to Book.

P/E Non Gaap TTM is 10.34 and P/E Non Gaap Fwd is 8.46, which implies an upside of 22% for the next 12 months.

Second, the "correct" price to book value "after acquisition" is 0.99 as of today, April 9th (I already discussed the difference vs the standalone ratio at the 0.7x level in the "thesis section"). The median of the sector is 1.09, which implies an upside of 10%. And if we add the ROE of 5-7%, we can deduce a target of 15-17% for the next 12 months.

So, averaging these two targets, I conclude my target at $33 per share, a 20% upside for the next 12 months.

data.title: Our Portfolio Managers Weigh Impact Of Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
Markets were already on edge, and sweeping new tariffs from President Trump have added another level of complexity. To help investors make sense of the implications, we asked our portfolio managers two key questions:

  1. How do the new tariffs impact your asset class?
  2. What indicators should investors be watching?

Their answers offer timely perspective on this shifting environment and provide valuable context to help investors cut through the noise.

Digital Assets: De-dollarization Moves Bitcoin Towards Monetary Role

Matthew Sigel

Impact

The Trump administration’s April 2 tariff package—targeting imports from China and the EU—has reignited global trade tensions and increased the risk of monetary and geopolitical fragmentation. Bitcoin initially dipped from $85k to the $81K range after the announcement and sold off further over the weekend, but has still outperformed the Nasdaq across every major timeframe—1 week, 1 month, YTD, and over the past 1, 2, 3, 5, and 10 years. While slower growth alone isn’t bullish for Bitcoin, the policy response might be: if tariffs weigh on GDP without triggering a fresh inflation spike, the Fed may have scope to cut rates—reintroducing the liquidity conditions under which Bitcoin has historically excelled. At the same time, the weaponization of trade and financial infrastructure continues to drive interest in neutral settlement rails.

That interest is no longer theoretical. China and Russia have reportedly begun settling some energy transactions in Bitcoin and other digital assets. Bolivia has announced plans to import electricity using crypto. And French energy utility EDF is exploring whether it can mine Bitcoin with surplus electricity currently exported to Germany. These are early signs that Bitcoin is evolving from a speculative asset into a functional monetary tool—particularly in economies looking to bypass the dollar and reduce exposure to U.S.-led financial systems.

Indicators to Watch

Investors should watch the evolving path of Fed policy: dovish shifts in rate expectations and rising liquidity are historically positive for Bitcoin. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) remains a key signal—any sustained dollar weakness may bolster the Bitcoin-as-hedge narrative, particularly in an environment of geopolitical fragmentation. Importantly, while 10-year Treasury yields surged on Monday, Bitcoin’s reaction was notably subdued. Unlike in 2022, rising yields did not trigger a wave of forced liquidations or volatility in crypto markets, suggesting that BTC may be decoupling from old macro sensitivities. Bitcoin ETP flows and on-chain activity also matter: despite recent volatility, U.S.-listed spot Bitcoin ETPs are still net positive by ~$600 million year-to-date, with renewed inflows in late March. Finally, watch for retaliatory moves from China or the EU—especially those aimed at bypassing dollar-based systems—which could accelerate adoption of crypto as an alternative settlement layer.

Gold: Safe Haven Demand Rises and Miners’ Margins Are Key

Imaru Casanova

Impact

Gold and gold stocks should ultimately benefit from the heightened level of risk across the global economy and global financial system. The unpredictability of economic policies and heightened market volatility should boost gold's appeal as the preferred safe-haven asset during times of global uncertainty. This should support a shift in investor sentiment towards gold and related equities.

Gold stocks’ leverage to the gold price, combined with their attractive valuations relative to the broader equity markets, and their low correlation with most other asset classes, should lead to a re-rating of the sector as investors look for a safer place to rotate capital to and as they look to diversify their portfolios.

Indicators to Watch

For the gold miners, investors should be focused on margins, assessing the impact of the ongoing trade war on global currencies and inflation trends for the sector. A weaker local currency leads to lower USD costs and improves margins for the miners. Gold miners were estimating cost inflation would average about 3-5% in 2025, prior to the recent tariff related developments.

Natural Resources: Sector Readies Response Playbook

Shawn Reynolds

Impact

Needless to say, the current tariff landscape is highly variable, complex and far from certain. Threats of further steps against China and their response ratchet up the intensity. Nevertheless, there is more clarity today than during February and March, even if it is still a blurry sight and worse than feared. However, given the severe reaction of global markets to tariff risks, we feel a large portion of the downside has been priced.

Undoubtedly, the biggest factor facing the entire natural resource sector is the current sentiment that a trade war will lead to a prolonged and deep global recession. In particular, the U.S. relationship with China has escalated and an off-ramp may take time and appear difficult to construct. Under that scenario, commodity demand of all of the main sectors covered by our Global Resources Strategy—energy (traditional and transitional), metals and mining and agriculture/paper/forest products—would suffer.

However, we believe the reality of application of “reciprocal” tariffs, as well as “retaliatory” tariffs will not be as disruptive as the worst fears. The broad-based 10% tariff influences the price of most commodities well within their recent ranges and is something to which the global economy historically has had a rather benign reaction relative to current perceptions. Additionally, geopolitical events over the last several years have reoriented and made supple commodity supply chains. The energy, metals and agricultural industries have adapted adroitly, and we believe these ecosystems will continuously evolve to newly optimal patterns. A good example of this is the 34% tit-for-tat tariffs on energy (mostly liquefied natural gas (LNG)) between China and the U.S. In our view, global LNG trade will adapt. We view that the delivery patterns of Russian, Iranian and Venezuelan crude oil has comfortably adjusted. In sum, most commodity producers have seen a version of this movie before and have a playbook for how to react.

Nevertheless, the net impact of recessionary fears have overwhelmed any moderation in the tangible impact of tariffs and uncertainty has spiked.

Indicators to Watch

As suggested above, the key factor to watch for our industries is how demand evolves—particularly in China. Most economists are now predicting a recession that should lead to an erosion of demand. But we would also watch several other macro factors including:

  • How credit markets ultimately react to this.
  • How quickly (or if) negotiations of tariffs settle and with whom.
  • Stimulus measures announced by major economies such as China or the EU.
  • The announcement of supportive positives for the U.S., including deregulation, tax relief, Federal Reserve easing and revamped fiscal spending.

Commodities: Weaker Dollar May Shift Tides After Demand Shock

Roland Morris

Impact

Very simple, commodities fell sharply because demand expectations collapsed. Most commodity indexes were close to all-time highs on April 2. The UBS Constant Maturity Commodity Total Return Index fell 6% in the following two days, a large move for a broad-based index. Copper, the commodity with a “PhD in economics”, declined 14% in two days after making new all-time highs in late March.

These tariffs are much higher and worse than expected. The outlook for global growth has collapsed. Gold has held up better than most commodity sectors but has pulled back about 4% from the new all-time highs reached on April 2. I believe that Trump’s global tariff war is very bullish gold.

Indicators to Watch

The dollar. I think that this trade war is very bearish the dollar in the longer term. If I am correct, commodities should be supported and could resume a bullish trend.

Emerging Markets Bonds: Spreads Pressured, EM Currencies Steady

Eric Fine

Impact

Tariffs and their associated recession risks are having three main effects on emerging market bonds.

  1. The “easy” one: spreads should widen on dollar-denominated bonds. Credit spreads are extremely correlated in risk-off moments like this, and long credit (from investment grade to high yield) has been the most consensus view, for arguably decades. We’d also note that corporate bonds can get incredibly illiquid and that this risk is almost always un-priced, further pressuring spreads higher.
  2. Risk-free yield curves should rally. This means Treasuries, as well as the risk-free yield curves of emerging market local-currency bonds (which were actually up on the year until April 4).
  3. Emerging market currencies have been resilient, only cracking recently when U.S. stocks deepened their declines. EM local currency is incredibly under-owned, which explains their strength this year (despite the selloff in recent days). They also have higher real rates and are often net creditors in dollars, so have FX intervention power. Also CNY has been kept unchanged by the Chinese authorities, anchoring EM currencies.

Indicators to Watch

CNY: watch the daily fix. If it stays largely unchanged (versus the daily model predictions), China is remaining a stabilizing force for emerging markets.

China stimulus: it’s the logical pressure on China, which was already moving in that direction.

The U.S. Federal Reserve: a dovish tilt could mark a turn in trend.

Also, watch the big asset prices combos for the U.S. In particular, if U.S. stocks are down and Treasuries are down, that’s very bad, as that indicates an exit from anything other than cash. If U.S. stocks are down and Treasuries are down and the USD is down, that’s very, very bad, as that is an exit even from cash and the U.S. system.

Emerging Markets Equity: Short-Term Challenges May Accelerate Structural Trends

Ola El-Shawarby, CFA

Impact

The U.S. administration’s proposed reciprocal tariff framework adds a new layer of complexity to the global trade outlook, particularly for export-oriented economies. However, rather than derailing momentum, it may accelerate structural trends already reshaping emerging markets—such as the shift toward domestic demand, innovation-led growth and supply chain diversification.

While tariffs pose short-term challenges for China, they reinforce the country’s strategic pivot toward consumption, high-value manufacturing and technological innovation—including advances in AI and next-generation industries—supported by targeted policy measures. India remains relatively insulated, with growth anchored by robust internal demand and ongoing reform. In Europe, the risk of trade disruption is being met with growing support for coordinated fiscal expansion.

With U.S. exceptionalism increasingly in question, the potential for a weaker dollar and lower rates reduces external pressures on emerging markets. That said, equity market volatility is likely to persist amid uncertain global growth and shifting policy dynamics. In this environment, selectivity is key—we remain focused on resilient, domestically driven businesses with strong fundamentals and clear exposure to long-term structural tailwinds.

Indicators to Watch

Several macro forces are shaping the outlook for emerging markets, and investors should stay focused on a few key areas:

  • Trade and policy dynamics: The broadening scope of U.S. tariffs raises the risk of retaliatory measures and further supply chain disruption, which could weigh on global trade and sentiment.
  • Inflation and interest rates: Trade-driven cost pressures may complicate the inflation outlook. However, a more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve—having paused its tightening cycle—supports the case for rate cuts, improving liquidity and easing pressure on EM currencies.
  • U.S. dollar trajectory: A weaker dollar would provide a meaningful tailwind for EMs by reducing external funding costs and supporting commodity-linked revenues.
  • Structural shifts: Ongoing trends such as nearshoring, supply chain diversification, and rising investment in domestic capacity remain central to EM growth stories.
  • Country-level fundamentals: In a more volatile and uncertain global environment, markets with sound external balances, credible policy frameworks, and scope for targeted stimulus are best positioned to navigate near-term risks and sustain long-term outperformance.

While short-term volatility is likely to persist, these indicators offer a useful lens through which to assess both risks and opportunities across the EM landscape.

Fixed Income: Spreads Widen as Confidence Indicators Flash

Fran Rodilosso

Impact

The newly introduced tariff regime equates to lower growth expectations, at best, and a higher risk of recession both in the U.S. and abroad as a bad, but not even worst, case. Slower growth and/or greater recession odds lead to higher credit spreads, and this impact is being felt across all risky debt markets, including investment grade credit, high yield, loans, CLOs and emerging markets debt.

There were many other market reactions in the couple of days following “liberation day” that are consistent with the rapid move wider in credit spreads, such as the rally in risk-free rates, the fall in commodity prices and, of course, equities entering bear market territory. We find all of these moves consistent with slower growth, lower earnings and—if there is not a rapid reversal in the tariff policies—multiple contraction. What is extremely interesting, and should be of some additional concern, however, is the bond market selloff on Monday, April 7. A correlated selloff with equities may show additional loss of confidence in the U.S. administration’s overall economic plan and its ability to manage through the multiple challenges of elevated prices, high and rapidly growing debt and economic uncertainty that the administration itself has created.

Indicators to Watch

In the credit space, we will be watching a variety of indicators:

  • The new issue market as an indicator of the health of capital markets.
  • Fund flows as a sign of investor sentiment and market technical.
  • Default rates in the high yield bond and leveraged loan markets as an indicator of financial stress, although these will take longer to develop.
  • Lastly, of course, the performance of the U.S. dollar and Treasuries as indicators of market confidence in the U.S. economy and U.S. institutions.

As always, we are looking for other signs of stress that could potentially lead to credit market dislocations, such as funding market disruptions and wholesale unwinding of leveraged positioning. So far, we have seen no such cracks emerge.

Municipal Bonds: As Costs Pressure Projects, Timing and Pricing Matter

Tamara Lowin

Impact

The cost of construction projects across all sectors will increase as tariffs raise the price of materials sourced internationally. Some projects will be shelved, but we expect to see a combination of strained financials as projects are completed over budget and increased revenue streams to close funding gaps—whether higher tolls, tuition costs, rent, healthcare services and/or other fees. Replacing aging infrastructure, from bridges to potholed roads, will be fiscally more difficult to justify. Expansion projects will also likely be stalled without increases in revenue streams, and areas could find themselves with insufficient resources, such as hospital beds and transit.

Public borrowers without expansion or replacement plans will also feel additional strain due to operational supplies and employee costs. Healthcare providers especially have already seen an increase in general supply costs, many sourced from overseas, and in addition, have attempted to address the U.S. shortage of nursing staff with international hiring, which will become more difficult, further squeezing margins and closing more beds.

Sales and use tax revenues are likely to remain within range due to increasing base prices balanced by lower anticipated tourism and domestic demand. Entities directly involved in international trade—such as ports, airports, energy and the state and local governments that depend on the revenue, economic activity and jobs from those facilities—will see strain from higher unemployment (lower income taxes and high unemployment costs) and lower revenue.

Indicators to Watch

Understanding the timing and any guaranteed pricing for existing construction projects as well as realistic pricing projections for future plans will be important to evaluate the feasibility of their success. The liquidity cushion of borrowers as well as revenue elasticity will also be a necessary factor to understand their pricing power.
